topic: Sanad offices are staffed entirely by Omani nationals — the only sector in Oman where 100% Omanisation is mandatory. Here is what that means for how they operate.
format: Open with a specific Sanad or PRO failure moment — a visa that expired, a client who found out at the airport, a missed MOL filing — make it so specific the reader stops scrolling
post:
The client caught it, not me.

He was standing at Muscat Airport at 7 AM, about to miss his flight, when he realized his work permit had expired three days earlier.

It was buried in a WhatsApp thread from two weeks ago. Forty other messages on top of it. Twelve different companies in the same chat.

He missed the flight. The company bought a new ticket.

That's what work permit tracking looks like in Oman when your "system" is a shared Excel file and a group chat.

Here's the actual job inside a Sanad office: you're the licensed, Omanisation-mandated connection between your clients and every government authority in the country. MOL filings. ROP clearances. Civil Status transactions. Omanisation certificates. All of it, across 20+ companies, coordinated mostly over WhatsApp.

Not because anyone's being reckless. Because nothing was ever actually built for this workload.

When you're holding 40 work permits, 15 visa renewals, and 8 MOL filings in your head at once, you need an alert that fires before the expiry — not after someone's already arguing with airport staff at the departure gate.

That's the gap SmartPRO Hub is built to close. And if you want to start right now, our free Sanad AI Assistant gives you instant answers on work permits, visas, and verified government fees — no login, no setup required.

Try it free: https://www.thesmartpro.io/sanad/assistant?utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=sanad-pro
